title: Credit scores: the number that follows you
description: Three digits that decide what borrowing costs you.
summaryPoints: A credit score (300–850) is your borrowing track record as one number
Payment history (35%) and amounts owed (30%) drive most of your score
Keep card usage under 30% of your limit — $300 on a $1,000 card
A low score makes the exact same loan cost thousands more
Build credit with a small card paid in full, on time, every month

content.title: Your financial GPA
content.body:
Lend a friend your bike and get it back late, muddy, with a flat — you'll think twice next time. Banks think the same way, except they keep receipts.

Your **credit score** is a number from 300 to 850 that sums up how reliably you've repaid borrowed money. High score: lenders compete for you. Low score: doors close and prices rise.

Think of it as your financial GPA — except this one follows you to loans, apartments, and sometimes jobs.

content.prompt: Credit scores usually run from 300 up to ___.
content.explanation: The standard scale tops out at 850. Around 650 is average; 750+ opens the best doors.

content.title: The report and the score
content.body:
Banks, landlords, and phone companies don't know you personally, so they check two things:
- **Credit report** — your full borrowing history: accounts, payments, slip-ups
- **Credit score** — that history compressed into one number

The report is the transcript; the score is the GPA. You can check both yourself for free — and you should, because reports contain mistakes more often than you'd think, and you can dispute errors.

content.prompt: Checking your own credit score lowers it.
content.explanation: Self-checks are 'soft' inquiries — totally harmless. Only hard inquiries from lenders (when you apply for credit) can shave off a few points.

content.title: The five ingredients
content.body:
Your score is a recipe with five ingredients:
- **Payment history (35%)** — do you pay on time? The biggest slice by far
- **Amounts owed (30%)** — how much of your available credit you're using. Under 30% of your limit is the sweet spot
- **Length of history (15%)** — older accounts mean more evidence
- **Credit mix (10%)** — handling different types (card + loan) responsibly
- **New credit (10%)** — lots of recent applications looks desperate

Notice what's NOT in there: your salary, your savings, your follower count.

content.prompt: Match each ingredient to its share of your score.
If you could only do ONE thing for your score, what should it be?
content.explanation: Payment history is 35% of the score — the single biggest factor. (Salary isn't even part of the formula.)

content.prompt: Your card's limit is $1,000. To follow the 30% rule, keep your balance under…
content.explanation: 30% of $1,000 = $300. Below that you look in control; near the limit you look maxed out and risky.

content.instructions: One word here is sabotage. Tap it.
content.sentence: To protect your credit history, close your oldest credit card.
content.explanation: Length of history is 15% of your score. Your oldest account is doing quiet, valuable work — let it live.

content.title: What the number costs you
content.body:
Same $15,000 car, same five-year loan:
- Score 780 → about 4% APR → roughly $1,600 in interest
- Score 600 → about 12% APR → roughly $5,000 in interest

That's $3,400+ extra for the exact same car.

It doesn't stop there: landlords check scores before renting to you, the best credit cards (cash back, travel perks) want high scores, and some employers review your credit report — though not the score itself — when hiring.

content.prompt: Roughly how much MORE does Sara pay for the same car?
content.context: Ali (score 780) gets a $15,000 car loan at 4%. Sara (score 600) gets the same loan at 12%. Both repay over five years.
content.explanation: Ali pays about $1,600 in interest; Sara pays about $5,000. Same car, same price tag — a $3,400 penalty for the lower score.

content.title: Building it from nothing
content.body:
No history yet? Everyone starts with zero evidence. The playbook:
- Start small: a student card or a secured card (you deposit money first)
- Put one small regular bill on it — a subscription works
- Pay the FULL balance on time, every month, forever
- Keep usage under 30% of your limit
- Don't close your oldest account — age helps

Boring? Completely. That's the point: lenders adore boring.

content.prompt: Build credit from zero. Put the moves in order.
steps: Open a student or secured card
Put one small monthly bill on it
Pay the full balance on time each month
Let the account age — don't close it
content.explanation: Start small, create a payment rhythm, then let time do the compounding. On-time history plus account age is the engine.

content.prompt: Does it help or hurt your score?

items.text: Ignoring errors on your credit report

content.title: Myths that wreck scores
content.body:
Two popular myths, both expensive:

**"You need to carry debt to build credit."** Wrong — you need payment HISTORY, not debt. Use a card, pay it off in full monthly. Interest paid: $0. History built: yes.

**"Paying the minimum means I'm fine."** It dodges late fees, but interest keeps piling onto the balance — you can pay every month and still sink deeper.

content.prompt: As long as you make the minimum payment, your balance can't grow.
content.explanation: Minimums avoid late fees, but interest can add more than you paid — the balance creeps UP while you technically 'stay current'.

content.title: Credit Score Adventure
content.intro: You're starting at 650 — dead average. Three moments, one goal: hit 750.

steps.situation: Your $50 phone bill is due on the 10th. It's the 8th — and concert tickets drop today.

options.text: Pay the bill now, tickets with what's left
options.feedback: On-time payment — that feeds the biggest score factor (35%).

options.text: Tickets first, pay the bill on the 15th
options.feedback: Five days late. Payment history takes the biggest, fastest hits.
A missed payment can sit on your report for years. Brutal trade for a concert.

steps.situation: Your first credit card arrives: $1,000 limit. The $200 sneakers you've wanted are right there.

options.text: Buy them, pay the full $200 when the bill comes
options.feedback: History built, $0 interest, utilization stays low. Textbook.

options.text: Buy them, pay only the $20 minimum
options.feedback: Interest starts growing on the rest, and the lingering balance raises your utilization.
A maxed-out card screams risk — amounts owed is 30% of your score.

steps.situation: You apply for an apartment and the landlord runs a credit check. A friend suggests: 'Open a few new cards first so you look richer.'

options.text: Apply as you are — your record speaks
options.feedback: On-time payments and low balances are exactly what landlords want. Approved!

options.text: Open 3 new cards the week before
options.feedback: A burst of applications looks desperate — and dings your score right when it's being checked.

options.text: Pay a site $99 to 'instantly boost' your score
options.feedback: Instant-boost services range from useless to scam. Nobody legit can rewrite your history overnight.

content.outro: 650 → 750 isn't luck: pay on time, keep balances low, don't chase new credit. Small boring moves, big number.
